We need a deep reorg of an internal admin dashboard for ExoPriors.  Current state: - Served by Rust API at internal.exopriors.com (root proxied to API). UI is static HTML + vanilla JS at src/api/src/routes/dashboard.html. - Data endpoints: /api/stats (queries + errors + latency), /api/alignment-load, /api/system, /api/pools, /api/margins, /api/page-views/stats, /api/queries/{recent,slow,errors,security}, /api/users, /api/feedback (stats/list), /api/forum-sync, /api/billing/balance, /api/billing/topups, /api/billing/rates, /api/page-views/{recent,top,visitors}. - UI currently has a top bar, a few summary cards, then multiple panels (Scry load, System, Margins, Page Views) and a tab bar that swaps a single large table for: billing, sync, costs, feedback, activity, latency, rejected, queries, accounts, traffic. - There’s a “detail mode” toggle that shows/hides sensitive tabs.  Request: - Deep reorg into a more intuitive layout, dense info (little padding), keep basically all info. Probably in Svelte but can remain HTML if serving constraints. Need a clear information hierarchy.  Ask: - Propose a new layout/IA (information architecture) for this admin panel: what sections, how to group data, recommended structure (grid/columns/panels), and interaction patterns (tabs/accordion/etc) to keep it dense but intuitive. - If you recommend Svelte or staying vanilla, say why given internal domain served by API. - Include a suggested visual layout (e.g., top KPI strip, left ops column, right biz column, query log panel with sub-tabs, etc).

Hope you are having a great day.

I canceled well in advance of Dec 1. I was not made aware of a 7 day notice. Money is tight. If my membership can just be cleanly canceled, there is a real chance I'll be able to come back to the YMCA. But if I am going to be accruing late fees for a service I cancelled and have not been able to use in months, I'm going to have to sever ties and just never be able to use YMCA again.

I hope institutions can still find flexibility in these tenuous and exciting times.

Best,
Xyra

remember that we are really trying to keep things simple. We're running hard with the simple idea of agent communication via attribute prompts. We're not trying to be too fancy. Of course, there will be really cool things to try out, like more sophisticated queries and ways of factorizing cognitive work.

We're not trying to go too crazy. We're aiming for a good re-ranking API and a system where LLMs can simply store their pairwise ratio judgments. There are fancier things we want to try, like them suggesting another attribute to rate things by.

There are more sophisticated protocols you could have for storing a judgment across agents. But we don't want to complicate it too much. We really want to work with foundational, simple stuff. We do want a robust system that can handle lots of writes and reads. I was doing this because I wanted a really elegant and robust API.

This would be a multi-stage system where some parts are functional and some are idempotent. We just want a good infrastructural API that people can seriously build on. This is the singularity.

We want to capture a lot of the wealth. We need very robust Rust APIs for agent communication. I want to keep some long-term ideas in the back of our minds, maybe protocols for agents communicating live or something.

We want to run with simple primitives: agent interoperable numbers, cache and SQL, and agent respawning. It should be simple. See if you can get a clear doc that's grounded and genuinely helps us move forward.

I recently shipped a novel tool, https://exopriors.com/scry, for giving the public unprecedentedly nuanced, ergonomic search power over many public datasets. The technology let's users, just by talking to Claude, safely run sophisticated, arbitrary SQL + vector queries on your own powerful, well-indexed servers.

If you'd be interested in giving users that sort of experience over your data, I'd be happy to chat about building that for you. I could configure any Linux servers for you, such as a network of Hetzner machines (very cheap monthly pricing), for about $30-$60k, plus your server costs.

Kind regards,

Xyra

The last 30 days we've processed 8015 SQL queries from 11 authenticated users (have some early power users doing a lot of deep research with their agents) as well as 2866 queries from 128 public IPs. I am currently running on 1 of Hetzner's max servers (AX162, 90 TB nvme, 1152 GB ram). This was ~$1500/month before the price hike that will start costing me ~$4K/month starting in April, so I will downgrade RAM then.

In terms of simple hardware goals, having runway on my one server would be great. I could also use another large server to get comfortable with horizontal scaling as well as moving faster (e.g. having a dev vs prod server and being more resilient to errant agent mishaps).

In terms of my goals, I want to do everything I need to do to ship premier epistemic infrastructure, and other canonical things you just need to do to navigate an intelligence explosion. Ideally that means run a $100M epistemic infra operation/lab. in the short term, that means polishing up scry.io, and shipping the more foundational openpriors.com, a platform for agent structured prior elicitation, reconciliation, and propagation. basically if you make agents communicate and hack away at  open-ended problems through narrow enough type signatures, you can really be saying that they _emit_ signal, and then with economic incentives, you basically have an exceedingly tractable seed of exceedingly powerful epistemic cognitive work economy.

Also, the primitive is so, like, embarrassingly simple. You know, I mean, of course, it... It's, you know... It does fancy things when scaled, right? Like, it does beautiful things when scaled, but the very basics of it is... Like, it's not as sexy as, like, transformers and all this fancy training.

okeydoke we spinning up the digitalocean droplet right now. Give me a clean quality specification that my AI agent can deeply iterate off. please do the whole thing. I will provide the digitalocean ip/domain, we can ssh into it. you are free to decide the best technical decisions. this is one idea for you to consider: 
  - Server: Self-hosted DigitalOcean droplet (not serverless)
  - Reverse Proxy: Caddy (automatic HTTPS, simple config)
  - Database: SQLite (simple, file-based, no separate DB server)
  - Deployment: Direct to VPS with systemd services

  API Layer

  - Framework: Hono (lightweight, fast, minimal)
  - Runtime: Node.js with native --watch for dev
  - Style: Minimal server - just routes for specific purposes, no over-abstraction

  CLI Tools

  - Language: TypeScript
  - Build: tsx for dev, tsc for production
  - CLI Framework: Commander
  - Testing: Vitest
  - Linting: ESLint

  Python Services

  - Framework: FastAPI
  - Package Manager: uv (fast, modern alternative to pip)
  - Server: Uvicorn

  General Principles

  1. Simplicity over scalability - SQLite over Postgres, VPS over k8s
  2. Minimal dependencies - Hono over Express, uv over pip
  3. Self-hosted - Own infrastructure, not locked to cloud providers
  4. File-based where possible - SQLite, local configs
  5. No over-engineering - Flat structures, direct solutions
  6. Standard tooling - Caddy for HTTPS, systemd for process management

CREATE TABLE binary_ratio_comparisons (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v4(),
    entity_a_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES entities(id),
    entity_b_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES entities(id),
    attribute_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES attributes(id),
    rater_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES raters(id),
    raw_output TEXT,
    ln_ratio_estimate DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL CHECK (
        ln_ratio_estimate = ln_ratio_estimate
        AND ln_ratio_estimate >= ln(0.001)
        AND ln_ratio_estimate <= ln(1000)
    ),
    weight DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL DEFAULT 1.0 CHECK (weight >= 0 AND weight <= 10),
    privacy_level privacy_level NOT NULL DEFAULT 'public'::privacy_level,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    CONSTRAINT different_entities CHECK (entity_a_id != entity_b_id),
    CONSTRAINT canonical_entity_order CHECK (entity_a_id < entity_b_id),
    CONSTRAINT unique_comparison UNIQUE (rater_id, attribute_id, entity_a_id, entity_b_id)
);

in the general direction of "empowering the the public with new capabilities they didn't have before", Scry offers:

1) giving the public full Postgres SQL expressiveness while also doing vector search. 

1) giving the public text-to-SQL+vector search/research experience, optimized for plausibly the highest ergonomics agentic intelligence system. With the optimized prompt and how the system tends to fire off dozens of heavy SQL queries in a first exploratory and then definitive manner to answer the user's question, it's arguably the most thorough lexical+semantic deep-research experience you can get over narrow domains (those indexed and embedded in your database).

2) it combines multiple public commons into one database, with embeddings. Big Query has siloed tables, doesn't have things like all of arXiv, doesn't have embeddings, and is not easy to use with modern agents driving the experience.

3) there is full prog

1) full programmatic vector functionality. That's far more than vector DB similarity 

4) We just used a $220 Hetzner server and Postgres. This is a replicable, affordable model that people can use over custom datasets 


giving users arbitrary readonly-SQL access to powerful production servers is fairly novel. Google BigQuery does it, but it doesn't combined many datasets, have embeddings + vector search for them, or have the chatbot, agent-first ergonomics.

We now have the decisive exact‑baseline check you asked for.

  On a 50k LW‑comment sample (20 random queries), I compared:

  - Exact 2048 fp32 (seq scan) vs ANN 2048 fp32
  - Exact 1024 f16‑norm (seq scan) vs ANN 1024 f16‑norm
  - Exact 2048 fp32 vs Exact 1024 f16‑norm (representation drift)

  Results:

  - ANN 2048 recall@50 vs exact 2048: 1.00 (min=1, max=1)
  - ANN f16‑norm recall@50 vs exact f16‑norm: 1.00 (min=1, max=1)
  - Exact 2048 vs exact f16‑norm overlap@50: avg 0.921 (min 0.84, max 0.98)

  So:

  - ANN itself is not the problem at this scale; it recovered the exact top‑50 perfectly for both representations.
  - The real difference is the representation: 1024‑f16‑norm changes the ranking relative to full 2048 fp32 (≈8–16% of the top‑50 differs on average).

  If you want, I can repeat this on a larger sample, on HN comments specifically, or with different subvector lengths (e.g., 1536).

  On Scry ergonomics:

  - If we keep the column as vector(2048) and only add an expression index, existing Scry SQL still works, but it won’t use the new index unless the query shape matches the
    expression (l2_normalize(subvector(...))::halfvec(1024)).
  - That’s the ergonomic trade‑off. You can mitigate it by:
      1. A small immutable SQL helper (e.g., embedding_prefix_1024_f16(v)), used in both the index and queries, or
      2. A view or function in alignment that exposes the indexed expression so agents can just call alignment.search() and get the right plan.
  - The embed endpoint can remain 2048‑f32. You’d just slice + normalize + cast inside SQL when you want the halfvec index to kick in.

  If you want, I can add the helper function + update alignment.search() to use it, so Scry stays ergonomic while we experiment.

posted to facebook last week: 
((formerly known by Evan Ward)). The technological singularity is coming. God-like superintelligent systems doing very unhuman things. Just as I started worrying about in 2016, just as I left West Point over in 2019, just as I came to Berkeley in 2020 to learn software engineering and build epistemic tools to do something about. 
If the key situations require virtually any coordination or conscientiousness to go well, we are on track to miserably fail. We don't even have infrastructure for SORTING SHIT OUT IN LISTS BY SUBTLE QUALITIES WITH LLMs. 
Literally you cannot find any rock solid code for even optimal query planning for incomplete pairwise ratio comparison matrices, and there's a hundred other tricks in the space of sorting shit out in lists by vibes with LLMs, that is foundational for any kind of real civilizational epistemic infrastructure (beyond whatever the fuck we are doing with the internet and growing thinking machines). But real epistemic infrastructure does start to give us options, does ramp up the civilizational conscientiousness, does help Opus-like (benign) LLMs move towards meaningfully factorizing key cognitive work at scale.
